condition.qualitative_description: Surface water body area is declining at more than 5 ha/year and fewer than 3 distinct water bodies exist in the MWS, indicating shrinking storage infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A declining trend in total SWB area combined with a low count of water bodies signals that surface storage infrastructure is contracting — through siltation, encroachment, or neglect — rather than expanding. In the sub-humid alluvial plains of Punjab, Haryana and western UP, tanks and ponds historically buffered rabi and zaid season irrigation demand. Their loss directly reduces the water available outside the monsoon window. Siltation is a well-documented cause of storage loss: Dashora et al. (2019) showed that a 5-year-old check dam in Gujarat had filled completely with silt at 40 cm/year, eliminating recharge capacity entirely.

condition.qualitative_description: The mean ratio of rabi-season SWB area to kharif-season SWB area is below 0.4, indicating that water bodies dry out rapidly after the monsoon and cannot sustain rabi or zaid irrigation.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A low rabi-to-kharif SWB area ratio means that surface water bodies hold water during the monsoon but lose it quickly thereafter, leaving farmers without surface irrigation sources for rabi wheat or zaid vegetables. Rapid post-monsoon drying is consistent with siltation reducing storage depth, poor embankment design, or high seepage losses. This is the seasonal failure mode most directly reported by farmers in the Indo-Gangetic plains as 'insufficient water for rabi'.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 5 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA for the MWS, indicating historically low public investment in water harvesting infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low MGNREGA investment in SWC and irrigation works means that existing tanks and ponds have not been desilted, repaired, or supplemented with new structures. Dashora et al. (2019) demonstrated that manual desilting of a check dam increased recharge by 84% over the preceding year, while neglected structures showed declining infiltration rates. In the alluvial plains context, desilting of tanks through MGNREGA is a low-cost, high-return intervention; its absence amplifies the storage deficit signalled by sig_01 and sig_02.

condition.qualitative_description: Nearest agricultural cooperative (PACS or marketing co-op) is more than 15 km away, limiting farmers' access to institutional credit for irrigation investment such as pump sets, pipelines, or micro-irrigation systems.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Distance to cooperative credit institutions constrains marginal and small farmers from investing in pump sets, drip or sprinkler systems, or pipeline conveyance that could compensate for failing surface storage. In the sub-humid alluvial plains, where groundwater is accessible but lifting costs are significant, credit access is a key enabler of irrigation resilience. Poor cooperative access amplifies the impact of infrastructure failure by preventing private adaptive investment.

condition.qualitative_description: Farmers report that well or tubewell depth has increased noticeably over recent years, or that pumping costs have risen due to lower water tables, particularly during rabi and zaid seasons when surface water is unavailable.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In alluvial aquifer settings, well depth data are not available from the framework's remote-sensing variables. However, farmer-reported increases in pumping depth or cost during the dry season indicate that groundwater is being drawn down to compensate for failing surface irrigation infrastructure. This is a secondary stress signal: the primary pathway is irrigation infrastructure failure, but it cascades into groundwater stress when farmers over-extract to compensate. User-supplied information on well depth trends is essential to distinguish this pathway from primary groundwater depletion.

overall_reasoning_note: Irrigation infrastructure failure in the sub-humid alluvial plains is confirmed when at least two of the three primary signals are present: declining SWB area trend with low water body count (sig_01), rapid post-monsoon drying of water bodies (sig_02), and low MGNREGA investment in water harvesting (sig_03). The cooperative distance signal (sig_04) and well depth signal (sig_05) are amplifying and contextual respectively. A diagnosis of irrigation_challenges should be preferred over groundwater_stress when SWB metrics are the dominant signal and SOGE or well depth data do not independently confirm aquifer depletion. Always verify whether canal command area covers the MWS — if a perennial canal is present and functional, the pathway shifts toward conveyance or distribution failure rather than storage deficit.

confounders.confounder: Primary groundwater stress (over-extraction from alluvial aquifer) causing water scarcity independently of surface infrastructure failure
confounders.how_to_distinguish: If SOGE classification is Semi-critical or above, or if well depth trend shows rapid deepening (>3 m over 8 years) even in years with normal rainfall, groundwater stress is the primary pathway. Irrigation infrastructure failure is more likely when SWB area is declining and NREGA investment is low but SOGE remains in the Safe category. Check whether the river_name variable indicates a perennial source — its absence strengthens the infrastructure gap hypothesis.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought reducing both surface storage and groundwater recharge, mimicking infrastructure failure
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare mean_annual_precipitation_mm against the AER-9 baseline of 740–960 mm. If precipitation has declined significantly (trend_annual_precipitation_mm < -10 mm/year) and drought_severe_return_period is short (<4 years), drought is a co-driver. Infrastructure failure is the primary pathway when SWB area declines even in years with normal or above-normal rainfall, indicating siltation or structural deterioration rather than reduced inflow.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers in this area noticed that they need to pump from greater depths than before, or that pumping costs have increased over the past 5–10 years? Approximately how deep are tubewells or borewells currently (less than 20 m, 20–50 m, or more than 50 m)?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If well depth has increased substantially or pumping costs have risen sharply during rabi/zaid seasons, this confirms that surface irrigation failure is forcing groundwater substitution, strengthening the irrigation_challenges diagnosis. If well depth is very deep (>50 m) and increasing rapidly even in normal rainfall years, the diagnosis should be reviewed for a co-occurring groundwater_stress pathway, and solar pump or micro-irrigation solutions become more urgent.

choices.label: Well depth has stayed roughly the same (within ~1 m)
Wells have deepened by more than 2–3 m in the last 5–10 years
Springs or shallow wells have dried up permanently or for much longer

sources.title: Hydrologic and cost benefit analysis at local scale of streambed recharge structures in Rajasthan (India) and their value for securing irrigation water supplies
sources.relevance_note: Documents siltation as a primary cause of check dam and tank storage failure, quantifies the 84% recharge improvement from manual desilting, and provides benefit-cost ratios for tank rehabilitation — directly supporting tank desilting as a solution and siltation-driven SWB area decline as a diagnostic signal.

sources.title: Climate Change 2014 – Synthesis Report
sources.relevance_note: Provides global context for increasing water scarcity under climate change, supporting the framing of irrigation infrastructure adequacy as a critical adaptation challenge in sub-humid agricultural regions.

sources.title: Global Monthly Water Scarcity: Blue Water Footprints versus Blue Water Availability
sources.relevance_note: Demonstrates that water scarcity is most acute on a monthly basis during dry seasons, consistent with the rabi and zaid season irrigation failure pattern described in this card for sub-humid plains.

sources.title: National Compilation of Dynamic Ground Water Resources of India 2022
sources.relevance_note: Provides SOGE classification thresholds and groundwater development status for Punjab, Haryana and western UP, relevant for distinguishing irrigation infrastructure failure from primary groundwater stress in the AER-9 context.
